And then for the Atlas walkthrough.
So just to kind of give you a quick overview.
So and then like a background.
So Atlas is our own in-house content generation platform that we launched, I would say, last April.

Before that, we were using Aerops and we have an entire team of in-house like software engineers who built this platform and continue improving it and both working with, you know, they're really working closely with our content editorial team to make sure that the workflows work exactly as expected.
And it has evolved so much since it first launched and it's constantly being improved.
So I want to show you first where all of the different artifacts live.
So at the beginning of our engagement, we created artifacts like company context.
Products and services, audience personas, and writing guidelines.
Those are the core artifacts that are really important for the articles to be accurate and in the tone that you want it to be.
However, we have also created custom artifacts like customer spotlights.
And this one includes, you know, key case studies that we want to reference in your content.
It has like, you know, it contains URLs.
It contains like the key problems and the outcomes that Panther enabled.
So that every piece of content that we create includes like proof points related to Panther that we can immediately reference.
We also have different personas here.
To kind of really make sure that when we're creating content for a specific persona, we are addressing the right fears.
We're bringing up the right capabilities.
And then we also have writing guidelines for competitor content.
So this is, we're going to be creating obviously a lot of.
Like listicles where we're comparing different solutions and versus articles.
And these types of guidelines only apply to those articles to make sure that this type of content follows very specific guidelines.
And the reason why we break up these writing guidelines is because if you give LLMs too much context, if we had this in your actual writing guidelines, the AI would just get overwhelmed.
And it wouldn't follow, I feel like maybe 50% of the writing guidelines because there's just a token limit to how much information you can give an LLM.
So these writing guidelines are only applied to the workflow where we're generating comparison or competitor content.
And then we also have different proofreader checklists.
The proofreader checklists are applied at the very end of the drafting stage.
And this is when the AI takes the funding.
Draft reads it and then compares it to the writing guidelines and kind of evaluates whether the content followed the writing guidelines.
And if it didn't, it makes adjustments.
And this is also because as we've been working with customers in different industries, we've noticed that sometimes the writing guidelines might be applied partially.
Sometimes because we're human beings, we don't realize that we're providing conflicting information to the LLM.
So we might have one instruction that conflicts with another, and then the LLM doesn't know what to do, and it doesn't do either of those things.

So we have this proofreader checklist at the end to make sure that we catch anything, whether that's punctuation, use of specific phrases that you don't want us to use, or just like a specific sentence structure.
The proofreader checklist catches that and makes fixes.
We also have an AI-isms editor.
This is something that we have.

In the sprints, we have a lead editor, her name's Sydney, and she's really an incredible editor, and her job is to make sure that this pipeline is working well and that the content is, you know, doesn't read as written by AI.
And this is something that she uses to eliminate those annoying phrases that come up in AI content.
So let's, for example, you know, we have like, you know, we have connection phrases that the content has to use.
We have like bad examples, we have good examples, you know, anything that we read a lot of AI content every day, and we know what AI likes to do.
So we try to create guidelines around that as well.
We also have examples of good content here for the AI to reference, to understand what is, you know, what kind of content it should emulate.
And we have like different other different artifacts here for context as well, to go to the actual pipeline.
We have multiple pipelines here.
So we will spin up test pipelines if we're experimenting with something.
So, for example, we have like this test version from one of our engineers.
When we are, you know, if we notice that something's not right, you know, the content isn't good, we will create a test pipeline to try a different approach without impacting the primary pipeline.
But the two main pipelines that we're using right now are the agentic article generation for comparison and competitor articles.
And then the agentic article generation that just uses, that covers just general topics like how-to guides and topics that are more informational in nature.
And this is the workflow.
So what we've done is we've broken up the article creation process into multiple chunks.
And it starts with the topic.
So we provide the article title.
We provide it with a slight, like a short direction, just so that the AI knows what the focus on.
So...
So...
We give it, obviously, the target keyword because we want to rank in Google Search.
And then the next step after this is the AI is going to run a research workflow.

So it takes the assignment direction, and then it generates a list of questions that it has to research and gather information on in order to create a very comprehensive research document that it's going to use in the content generation process.

I don't know if you've ever tried to create an article, maybe in Claude or ChatGPT, and you didn't give it a lot of information, and you would notice the articles are very short, and they just repeat the same information over and over again.
And that's because it doesn't have enough research to go off of.
So with this step, we really make sure that the content is not just informative, but also high quality.
We use Tavilli as our researcher.
They're really advanced.
We also have EXA, if we notice that Tavilli isn't doing very well.
But the reason why our content...
And can be so in-depth and provide the right amount of information for even very complex audiences, it's because of this researcher.
Like, it's incredibly important to have, like, a good researcher in the mix.
After the researcher is done, then the article draft takes our assignment direction, and then it creates the first version of the article.
After this, we add the internal links programmatically, but we also do this manually at the very end.
We also add source links to any third-party, like, references that we used.
We also have the proofreader checklist.
Like I mentioned, we make sure that the writing guidelines were applied.
It fixes anything that wasn't applied.
Then we create the meta tags for SEO.
Keep in mind that sometimes we will modify this depending on what the article ends up covering.
Like, we use our own judgment here.
We have the fact checker.
The fact checker takes, breaks up the article.
to different chunks, and then it verifies the accuracy of each chunk using the researcher workflow.

So it's going to, if there's, for example, a paragraph that says that 30% of, I don't know, security developers use this tool, it's going to search and scrape the internet to actually see, okay, is there any type of, you know, information about on the internet that confirms this?
If it cannot confirm or find a valid source, it's going to rewrite that information.
However, the fact checker here is here very much as a kind of a safeguard.
We have found that our researcher does a really good job, and the fact checker doesn't even have to do that much in terms of, you know, correcting the information.
And we also have our human editors.
So after we run the fact checker, our editor is going to read this and verify any third-party links that we have, anything that, you know, comes up as, you know, if we notice any additional aid.

AI-isms or just issues with flow, we will fix that manually, so every article that we send to you, we read it from top to bottom, that's really important for me to emphasize, and the cover image we generated at the end, and that's when we will send it to you for review.
